title: Bio and Envs program review 19-20 (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
Program: BIOLOGY AND ENVIRONMENTAL SCIENCES FOR AY 2018/19
Date: February 28, 2020
Faculty Completing Form: VARTY, CLARKE, and KLEIN
1. Describe annual progress / changes / events in the program.
We think of our department as having three different components, our pre-health courses,
general education courses, and majors courses and degrees. Thus, we have split this analysis
up into those three groups.
Pre-Health
• Ann Klein started as our third FT faculty in spring of 2019, and thus far has
taught anatomy, physiology, microbiology, bioprep and intro to biology.
• Anatomy was offered online for the first time in fall of 2019. It filled in both
fall and spring with wait lists. It might be possible to successfully offer and fill
additional sections. But if we go that route we need to deal with finding a
way to staff the F2F classes that Clarke currently teaches.
• Enrollment in Microbiology (BIO 2600) appears to have stabilized at one
section per semester, similar to Human Physiology (BIO 2800). We do not
anticipate any increases in interest in the course such that would warrant the
addition of a second section.
• We are exploring replacing cadavers with digital and virtual resources. But
for now, we need to continue purchasing cadavers (see attached CQIP), as
the Willed Body Program is demanding them back within a specified time
frame.
• We are exploring attaching prerequisites to Microbiology. Most California
community colleges have either general biology and/or general chemistry
prerequisites for their microbiology courses.
• New adjunct faculty member Brian Criswell has taught two sections of
anatomy.
General Education
• The total number as well as the number of hybrid and online GE courses
offered in AY 15-16, 16-17, 17-18, and 18-19 and 19-20 are listed in the table
below. The numbers show that we have tripled our general education

-- 1 of 4 --

offerings since AY 15-16. Additionally, while we offered no online or hybrid
GE offerings in AY 15-16, we offered 5 online or hybrid courses in AY 19-20.

• While all offerings ’went’, some sections did not fill to capacity; we may have
reached a limit in the F2F offerings, but feel that there are additional
opportunities for online and hybrid
• After losing one ’maxed out’ adjunct faculty teaching GE over the summer we
are due to lose yet another next academic year. This will present a
challenge; unless we find new adjunct instructors, we anticipate being able
to offer less GE courses.
• We’ve filled ENVS1002 online in each semester and, as with anatomy,
suspect that we could fill more sections, but we have the same staffing
obstacle to adding more ENVS online sections as with Anatomy.
• We are exploring diversifying our GE course offerings. Klein is interested in
developing a 3-unit online lecture course on Microbiomes. Varty is interested
in collaborating with Mark Clifford to develop a 3-unit hybrid in Field Zoology
3.
• Trees and shrubs was offered in the fall and did not gather interest.
Majors
• We continue to offer one Biology course geared toward science majors each semester.
Enrollment in our General Biology courses (BIO 2100 and BIO 2200) had been stable, but
enrollment has dramatically increased this year perhaps a reflection of increase interest
in STEM. We will continue to monitor the numbers to ensure we can accommodate all
students who need to complete this course sequence.

-- 2 of 4 --

title: Bio and Envs program review 19-20 (Part 3)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
• The Environmental Science major is increasing in popularity. Varty struggles to help all
students meet the Work Experience requirement and is working to broaden her
network of local environmental scientists to continue to be able to help students gain
valuable work experience and meet this degree requirement.
Continued professional growth
• We continue to seek out opportunities to develop professionally but find it
increasingly difficult to do so on the FLEX budget we are allotted. Examples
of our professional growth activities during AY19-20, some of which cost us a
considerable amount of personal funds, include:
➢ As part of his sabbatical Clarke took a full set of Online Design
coursework thru @ONE and attended the HAPS conference
➢ Alison completed the @ONE Designing Dynamic Discussions
course, attended the Northern California Botany conference
at CSU Chico, completed the @ONE Peer online course
Reviewer training and has served as a Peer Reviewer for COS
ADJ courses
➢ Ann is participating in a STEM Faculty Learning Program
focused on pedagogical theory and implementation of active
learning techniques in the classroom. She will also complete
the @ONE Introduction to Online Teaching & Learning course
this spring.
2. Describe progress on assessing SLOs and PLOs for the program.
SLOS are being accessed in all biology and environmental science offerings. We have no
‘program’ SLOS, and so are not assessing any.
3. Discuss any support or obstacles encountered by the program.
• Recruiting and retaining adjunct faculty. We have lost many quality and ’maxed out’
adjunct faculty to other opportunities and retirement over the past three years (Bill
Odegard is leaving after spring 2020, Eveline Robinson, Ariel Halpern, Logan Smith, Joe
Champagne) and have been unable to find replacements. Bringing Ann on has helped,
as has recruiting Brian Criswell and Mark Clifford, but we still face staffing challenges.
• No department credit card for our Technician. Having a credit card would streamline
our purchases and require less work by the business and instructional offices. She is
extremely busy; this may free up some of her time to allow her to provide our students
with more support. This concern carries over from last year, it has not been addressed.

-- 3 of 4 --

title: Bio and Envs program review 19-20 (Part 4)
text:
College of the Siskiyous Annual Program Update
• Delayed budgetary information. We are not notified of our budget until well after the
semester starts, making prioritization and obtaining supplies for the first labs of the
semester problematic. This concern carries over from last year, it has not been
addressed.
• Timing of instructional equipment requests. Non-instructional areas are allowed to
request funds at any time of year, while we are limited to CQIPS submitted with
program review. This concern carries over from last year, it has not been addressed.
• Computer access. Increasingly, many laboratory activities require computer access. The
ASC computers are seldom available. We asked for a full set of 24 to ’equip’ one full lab
class; we received 9. Thus, we have included a CQIP for 15 more lap top computers.
• Professional development. Funds for professional development are inadequate. This
concern carries over from last year, it has not been addressed.
• Work Experience. We mentioned above that Biology Work Experience is required for
Environmental Science students. We feel this experience and the related connections to
local environmental scientists is crucial for our graduates. However, the turnover in the
Work Experience Coordinator position makes helping students obtain these experiences
difficult. We highly recommend that the college invest significantly in this program.

-- 4 of 4 --
